app_understanding.note: This is a code-system spec review. The 'application' is the dot-claude orchestration harness, not a web app. No Playwright testing was performed. All findings are code/spec-based.

live_testing.note: Not applicable — reviewed Python/bash script orchestration harness, not a browser-rendered application.

console_errors_checked.waiver: Non-browser system. Bash/Python script harness; no console runtime.

network_requests_analyzed.waiver: Non-browser system; no HTTP network requests to analyze.

pages_visited.waiver: Non-browser system. Code review covered 12 source files.

security_patterns_checked.note: Reviewed sensitive-data exclusion spec (Risk 7), auth not applicable to this CLI harness.

performance_benchmarked.waiver: Non-browser system; no page load metrics. Timeout budgets (5min/15min) reviewed at spec level.

issues.title: blast-radius-map.json dependency in Step 7.5 is a non-guaranteed upstream artifact
issues.description: Step 7.5 spec states: 'extracts focused subgraph based on BA's blast-radius-map.json'. The blast-radius-map.json is produced by BA Step 9 (blast-radius-tool.py Phase 1), but ba.md does not unconditionally guarantee its presence. It requires complexity_tier >= STANDARD or explicit invocation. For MICRO/SMALL tier tasks, Step 9 is explicitly skipped. If BA classifies the task as MICRO/SMALL, the blast-radius-map.json will not exist when Step 7.5 fires, and the graphify subagent has no defined fallback for this case.
issues.measured: ba.md Step 9 invokes blast-radius-tool.py conditionally; MICRO/SMALL tiers skip it. Spec Step 7.5 references blast-radius-map.json without a guard for its absence.
issues.expected: Step 7.5 spec must define behavior when blast-radius-map.json is absent: either (a) skip subgraph extraction and fall back to full-graph summary, or (b) require BA to always produce the map when graphify is enabled, or (c) document the nil-map path in the failure state machine.
issues.observation_notes: Verified by reading agents/ba.md §Complexity Tier Assessment (MICRO/SMALL skip Step 9) and spec Step 7.5 blast-radius-map.json reference at spec line 78.

issues.title: 'graphify' agent type not registered in CP_AGENTS or ALLOWED_AGENTS — checkpoint enforcement will fail open
issues.description: The graphify subagent dispatched in Step 7.5 operates as a full Claude subagent. However, 'graphify' is absent from CP_AGENTS in hooks/pretool-cp-checkin.py and from ALLOWED_AGENTS in scripts/spec-check.py. Without registration: (1) the dev-registry sentinel read FIRST ACTION pattern cannot enforce the graphify subagent's registration; (2) subagentstop-cp-enforce.py will not enforce checkpoint marking for the graphify agent; (3) spec-check.py mark/waive calls will be refused with 'unknown agent'. The spec's implementation plan lists hooks/pretool-cp-checkin.py and scripts/spec-check.py as files to modify (PR1), but does not call out CP_AGENTS and ALLOWED_AGENTS explicitly as required additions.
issues.measured: CP_AGENTS set at pretool-cp-checkin.py:60 does not include 'graphify'. ALLOWED_AGENTS tuple at scripts/spec-check.py:45 does not include 'graphify'. DEV_REGISTRY_AGENTS = CP_AGENTS (same set).
issues.expected: 'graphify' added to both CP_AGENTS (pretool-cp-checkin.py) and ALLOWED_AGENTS (spec-check.py) before PR1 is merged, consistent with how 'test-writer' was added at spec-20260518-225715.
issues.observation_notes: Confirmed by reading pretool-cp-checkin.py lines 60-77 and scripts/spec-check.py lines 45-61. 'test-writer' was added to both sets together per spec-20260518-225715 §5.2 as the precedent.

issues.title: Decimal step numbering (Step 1.5, Step 7.5) violates existing integer-step style standard
issues.description: The spec introduces 'Step 1.5' and 'Step 7.5' as the canonical names for the two integration touchpoints. However, commands/dev.md and agents/dev.md both enforce 'CRITICAL: No decimal step numbering (use sequential integers: Step 1, Step 2, Step 3, NOT Step 1.1, Step 1.2)'. The spec itself acknowledges this in Risk 1: 'No decimal Step headings in files (style-inspector rejects) — use between Step N and Step N+1'. The risk note says to use 'between Step N and Step N+1' in wording, but the spec, orchestrator view, and all agent views use '1.5' and '7.5' as literal headings throughout. When PR2 modifies commands/dev.md, style-inspector will reject decimal headings.
issues.measured: Spec uses 'Step 1.5' and 'Step 7.5' as headings in the spec body, all five views (orchestrator, architect, ba, dev, qa), and the Risk Checklist. agents/dev.md Quality Checklist line 629 explicitly forbids decimal step numbers.
issues.expected: All implementation deliverables (commands/dev.md, dev-command.md, agents/*.md) use wording consistent with the existing integer-step scheme: 'between Step 1 and Step 2' / 'Pre-BA hydration' and 'between Step 7 and Step 8' / 'Pre-DEV enrichment', not '### Step 1.5' or '### Step 7.5'. Risk 1 acknowledges this but the spec itself is not yet compliant.
issues.observation_notes: Risk 1 in spec acknowledges the constraint. Confirmed that integer-only rule appears in agents/dev.md Quality Checklist and commands/dev.md patterns.

issues.title: Step 1.5 Bash call from orchestrator violates Orchestrator-Prompt-Purity hook (pretool-orchestrator-prompt-purity.py)
issues.description: Step 1.5 is specified as 'Orchestrator directly calls graphify-query.py via Bash (NOT a subagent)'. The orchestrator is the main agent thread. CLAUDE.md states that non-whitelist tools are allowed once TOTAL per tool name per turn. Bash is whitelisted, so the call is allowed. However, the pretool-orchestrator-prompt-purity.py hook blocks Agent dispatches whose prompts contain shell syntax or tool names. The concern is the opposite direction: when the orchestrator writes the Step 1.5 Bash call as part of a slash-command's markdown instructions, the orchestrator IS executing Bash directly — but commands/dev.md already does this in Step 1 for DEV_SESSION_ID generation. This pattern is established, so the Bash call itself is not blocked. The issue is that CLAUDE.md 'Orchestrator-Only Rule' whitelist includes Bash, so the call is permitted. No violation here — this is a confirmed safe pattern.
issues.measured: CLAUDE.md Whitelist: 'Bash' is explicitly whitelisted for the orchestrator. commands/dev.md Step 1 uses Bash for DEV_SESSION_ID generation as the established pattern. The spec's 'NOT a subagent' design intent aligns with this.
issues.expected: Confirmed safe. Noting for BA awareness: the graphify-query.py Bash call from the orchestrator is architecturally consistent with the existing pattern for hook-initialization Bash calls in Step 1 of dev.md.
issues.observation_notes: Verified by reading CLAUDE.md Orchestrator-Only Rule whitelist and commands/dev.md Step 1 Bash initialization pattern. Low-confidence finding — safe pattern confirmed, but BA should document that graphify-query.py Bash is an orchestrator-direct call, not subject to subagent hook discipline.

issues.title: Concurrent write safety for global cache relies on file lock whose scope is underdefined
issues.description: Risk 3 states 'Global cache concurrent writes — file lock on /var/tmp/claude-graphify/<repo_key>'. The spec does not define: (a) which file is locked (a dedicated lock file? the manifest.json itself?), (b) the locking mechanism (fcntl.LOCK_EX, flock, a .lock sentinel file), (c) behavior when the lock is held longer than expected (timeout strategy), or (d) whether the Step 1.5 read-only path also acquires a shared lock to avoid reading a partially-written graph. The existing spec-check.py uses fcntl.LOCK_EX on the JSON file itself (scripts/spec-check.py:171), which is the existing locking pattern in this codebase. The spec should explicitly adopt this pattern or document its divergence.
issues.measured: Spec Risk 3 mentions 'file lock' without specifying mechanism. scripts/spec-check.py:171 uses fcntl.LOCK_EX on the JSON file as the established locking pattern. No lock specification exists in the storage layout or failure strategy sections.
issues.expected: graphify_lib.py (the shared library in PR1) should specify: lock file path, locking mechanism (fcntl.LOCK_EX consistent with spec-check.py), timeout (e.g., 30s before degraded), and read-path lock behavior (shared lock or no-lock with stale-read tolerance).
issues.observation_notes: Compared against spec-check.py locking at line 171 and 213 which uses fcntl.flock(LOCK_EX). The global cache locking spec is underspecified relative to the codebase's own established pattern.

issues.title: Context.json patching by graphify subagent creates write-after-read race against the orchestrator
issues.description: Step 7.5 states the graphify subagent 'Patches context-{ts}.json with graph_context field'. The context-{ts}.json file is written by BA, read and validated by the orchestrator in Step 5, read by QA in Step 6 (BA-QA validation), read again by the orchestrator in Step 7 BA-QA iteration, and then read by the graphify subagent in Step 7.5 to patch it. After patching, dev reads the same context file. This is a sequential pipeline so there is no concurrent race in normal flow. However, the spec does not define whether graphify writes an in-place patch (JSON parse + merge + overwrite) or a separate graph_context sidecar file. In-place patching a shared artifact creates auditability concerns: QA validates the pre-patch context, dev consumes the post-patch context — the file that QA validated is no longer the file dev receives. This is a provenance gap, not a runtime crash.
issues.measured: Spec states 'Patches context-{ts}.json with graph_context field' (line 79). No separate sidecar design is mentioned. QA validates context-{ts}.json before the patch; dev consumes the post-patch version.
issues.expected: Either: (a) graphify writes a separate sidecar file (e.g., context-{ts}-graph.json) and dev.md instructs dev to merge both, preserving QA-validated context integrity; or (b) graphify patches in-place and the spec explicitly documents that context-{ts}.json after Step 7.5 is a superset of the QA-validated version (graph_context field is additive-only, never modifies existing fields).
issues.observation_notes: Established pattern in this codebase: blast-radius-map.json is a separate artifact, not patched into context.json. Graphify's chosen in-place-patch model diverges from that pattern.

issues.title: PR ordering constraint: PR2 depends on PR1 library, but PR1 modifies hooks that run in the existing pipeline
issues.description: PR1 modifies hooks/pretool-cp-checkin.py, hooks/prompt-workflow.py, and scripts/spec-check.py. These hooks fire on every /dev invocation immediately after PR1 merges, before PR2 (which inserts the actual Step 1.5/7.5 workflow). If prompt-workflow.py in PR1 attempts to 'init graphify.json sentinel' and graphify-query.py does not yet exist (it is also in PR1 but may be merged in a different order), the sentinel init will fail. The spec says PR1 is 'independent, no behavior change' — but prompt-workflow.py modification to 'init graphify.json sentinel' IS a behavior change that fires on every UserPromptSubmit. This claim needs to be backed by a graceful no-op path when graphify-query.py is absent.
issues.measured: PR1 implementation plan at spec line 166 states: 'hooks/prompt-workflow.py (modify — init graphify.json sentinel)'. spec line 156 claims PR1 is 'independent, no behavior change'. These are contradictory if graphify.json sentinel init fires on UserPromptSubmit for all commands.
issues.expected: The prompt-workflow.py modification must be gated on CLAUDE_GRAPHIFY_ENABLED and graphify binary presence before any sentinel init fires, OR the sentinel init must be deferred to PR2 when the feature flags are also introduced in settings.json. PR1 should be strictly additive (new files, schema extensions) with zero hook behavior changes.
issues.observation_notes: Confirmed by reading spec Implementation Plan at lines 156-170. prompt-workflow.py is listed in PR1 as a modification. CLAUDE_GRAPHIFY_ENABLED is listed in PR2 (settings.json). The feature flag that guards hook behavior is introduced after the hook modification that needs the flag.

issues.title: Sensitive data exclusion spec (Risk 7) has no enforcement mechanism defined
issues.description: Risk 7 states 'Sensitive data in graph cache — exclude .env, credentials, keys, logs'. The spec names the risk but does not define where the exclusion list is enforced: in graphify-maintain.py, in the graphify CLI tool's --exclude flags, in graphify_lib.py, or in a configuration file. If the enforcement is delegated to the graphify CLI itself, the spec must verify that the external tool supports path exclusion patterns. If enforcement is in graphify_lib.py, the exclusion list must be defined there. The current spec does not indicate which component owns this responsibility.
issues.measured: Risk 7 names the exclusion need without specifying enforcement component. blast-radius-tool.py:35 provides the established pattern: EXCLUDE_FRAGMENTS tuple in the Python script itself, applied via in_scope() at line 47. No similar pattern is defined for graphify.
issues.expected: BA spec must specify: (a) which component enforces the exclusion (graphify_lib.py preferred, consistent with blast-radius-tool.py EXCLUDE_FRAGMENTS pattern), (b) the canonical exclusion list (.env, *.key, *.pem, credentials.*, logs/, .git/), and (c) whether the graphify CLI's own exclusions are sufficient or must be supplemented.
issues.observation_notes: Compared against blast-radius-tool.py:35 EXCLUDE_FRAGMENTS as established exclusion pattern. graphify spec does not replicate or reference this pattern.

issues.title: failure state machine missing transition table: what triggers degraded vs failed vs unavailable
issues.description: The spec defines 5 states: ok | degraded | failed | unavailable | skipped. It maps two specific transitions: 'CLI not installed → unavailable; parse errors → degraded; no cache → unavailable'. Three states lack transition definitions: 'ok' (what conditions produce it?), 'failed' (distinct from degraded how?), and 'skipped' (when CLAUDE_GRAPHIFY_ENABLED=0 or --no-graphify only?). The missing transitions mean dev must invent them, risking inconsistency between Step 1.5 (graphify-query.py) and Step 7.5 (graphify-enrich.py).
issues.measured: Spec at line 124-128 defines 5 states and 3 transitions. States 'ok', 'failed', and 'skipped' have no defined transition conditions. The spec is an input-less state list, not a state machine.
issues.expected: Complete state machine for graph_context.status: ok (graphify ran, cache populated, subgraph extracted without error), degraded (ran but output incomplete — parse errors, token cap exceeded, partial subgraph), failed (ran but threw an unhandled exception), unavailable (binary not installed OR no cache exists), skipped (CLAUDE_GRAPHIFY_ENABLED=0 OR --no-graphify flag OR task tier MICRO excludes it).
issues.observation_notes: The existing failure strategy at spec line 124 is a partial state enumeration. No state transition diagram or table exists in the spec.

issues.title: /dev-command parity (Risk 8) is under-specified: structural_context injection path not described for dev-command flow
issues.description: Risk 8 states '/dev-command parity — must sync structural_context and graphify enrichment'. commands/dev-command.md has a distinct workflow from commands/dev.md (it adds Three-Party Architecture, command development best practices). The spec's Implementation Plan lists dev-command.md as a PR2 modification but does not describe how the Step 1.5 / Step 7.5 insertions differ for dev-command.md's step numbering scheme relative to dev.md's scheme. dev-command.md may have different step numbers for its BA and DEV dispatch steps.
issues.measured: Risk 8 names the parity requirement. PR2 lists 'commands/dev-command.md (modify — sync graphify flow)'. dev-command.md header indicates a distinct workflow. No diff between dev.md and dev-command.md insertion points is given in the spec.
issues.expected: BA spec for PR2 should identify the exact step labels in dev-command.md that correspond to 'between Step N and Step N+1' for the 1.5 and 7.5 insertions, confirming that dev-command.md's BA dispatch step and DEV dispatch step have step numbering compatible with the graphify insertion design.
issues.observation_notes: Read dev-command.md header to confirm distinct workflow. The parity risk is real but the BA implementation plan is where the specific insertion points should be defined.

cp-01.action: Validate dual-touchpoint architecture: Step 1.5 deterministic Bash hydrator and Step 7.5 graphify subagent
findings: Step 1.5 design (orchestrator Bash, not subagent) is consistent with existing Bash-whitelist pattern in CLAUDE.md and Step 1 of dev.md.
Step 7.5 graphify subagent design requires 'graphify' registration in CP_AGENTS and ALLOWED_AGENTS (see arch-2).
blast-radius-map.json dependency in Step 7.5 is conditionally absent for MICRO/SMALL tier tasks (see arch-1).
context.json patching model diverges from the sidecar-artifact pattern used elsewhere in the pipeline (see arch-6).

cp-02.action: Verify storage layout: global cache at /var/tmp/claude-graphify and per-task artifacts in dev-registry
findings: Storage layout is consistent with existing dev-registry/{task_id}/ pattern for per-task artifacts.
/var/tmp/claude-graphify is on disk (not /dev/shm) — consistent with spec note and appropriate for persistence across sessions.
Concurrent write safety for the global cache is underspecified (see arch-5): no lock mechanism is defined, contrasting with fcntl.LOCK_EX used in spec-check.py.
manifest.json at the cache root is the correct approach for branch/HEAD/version tracking to address cache cross-branch pollution Risk 2.

cp-03.action: Verify failure strategy state machine with 5 states: ok, degraded, failed, unavailable, skipped
findings: 5 states named: ok, degraded, failed, unavailable, skipped.
Only 3 transitions are specified: CLI not installed → unavailable, parse errors → degraded, no cache → unavailable.
States 'ok', 'failed', and 'skipped' have no defined triggering conditions (see arch-9).
The 5-min / 15-min timeout values are specified but timeout transition (which state results from timeout?) is not defined.

cp-04.action: Validate cache cross-branch pollution prevention via manifest recording branch and HEAD and graphify_version
findings: manifest.json with branch, HEAD, graphify_version, file_hashes is the correct design for cross-branch pollution prevention (Risk 2).
The spec does not define what action graphify-query.py (Step 1.5, read-only) takes if manifest records a different branch than the current HEAD: skip? stale-read? or trigger a refresh request?
graphify-maintain.py handles the actual cache refresh (init + update). The boundary between read-only Step 1.5 and write-capable Step 7.5 is correctly separated.

risk_1_decimal_steps.note: Risk 1 acknowledges the constraint but the spec body and all 5 views use decimal headings. Implementation must use prose wording, not heading syntax. See arch-3.

risk_2_cache_cross_branch.note: manifest.json with branch + HEAD + graphify_version covers this. One gap: undefined behavior in Step 1.5 read path when manifest branch differs from current branch.

risk_3_concurrent_writes.note: File lock is mentioned but mechanism, lock file path, and timeout are unspecified. See arch-5.

risk_4_token_cap.note: 2000 hard cap is defined. Token counting mechanism (by what unit? tiktoken? character count?) is not specified but this is implementation detail deferred to graphify-query.py.

risk_5_dev_runs_graphify.note: dev.md will explicitly prohibit. This is a documentation control, not a technical enforcement. Acceptable for v1 given the advisory nature of graphify.

risk_6_advisory_vs_ambiguity.note: Spec clearly distinguishes: tool failure = advisory (never blocks), requirement ambiguity = blocking (BA must clarify). The distinction is unambiguous.

risk_7_sensitive_data.note: Named but enforcement component is not specified. See arch-8.

risk_8_dev_command_parity.note: Named as a risk, listed in PR2 files, but the specific insertion points in dev-command.md are not mapped. See arch-10.

pr1_independence_claim.detail: PR1 claims 'independent, no behavior change'. The modification to hooks/prompt-workflow.py to 'init graphify.json sentinel' fires on every UserPromptSubmit — this IS a behavior change. The feature flag (CLAUDE_GRAPHIFY_ENABLED) that should guard this behavior is introduced in PR2. This sequencing means PR1 introduces hook behavior that fires before the flag controlling it exists. See arch-7.
pr1_independence_claim.recommended_fix: Move prompt-workflow.py modification to PR2 alongside settings.json, OR make the sentinel init unconditionally no-op when graphify binary is absent (status=unavailable path fires silently).

pr2_depends_on_pr1.detail: PR2 modifies commands/dev.md to insert Steps 1.5/7.5, which call graphify-query.py and dispatch graphify subagent — both defined in PR1. This dependency is correctly ordered.

pr3_independence.detail: PR3 is documentation, .gitignore, and pull command update. No behavioral dependencies on PR1/PR2 ordering beyond needing PR2 to be merged first for documentation accuracy.

clean_areas: Dual-touchpoint separation of concerns (Bash hydrator vs subagent enricher) is architecturally sound and consistent with the orchestrator-only rule
Failure strategy advisory model (tool failure never blocks DEV) is appropriate and cleanly separates technical failure from semantic ambiguity
Storage layout separation (global /var/tmp cache vs per-task dev-registry artifacts) follows established patterns
Feature flag design (CLAUDE_GRAPHIFY_ENABLED=auto|1|0 + --no-graphify) is complete and follows the existing conditional-enable pattern
BA Reference Resolution Rule and new fail gates are well-scoped additions to existing BA-QA validation flow
QA graph_verification field is clearly additive and does not change existing QA pass/fail logic
manifest.json approach for cache cross-branch pollution prevention (Risk 2) is correctly designed
Advisory vs blocking distinction (Risk 6) is unambiguous and correctly implemented
